statement.markdown: Given a sorted array `nums` and a target value `target`, return `true` if the target exists in the array, else return `false`. The `binary_search` function should do a binary search.
statement.inputFormat: The first line contains a single integer `n` representing the size of array. The next line contains `n` spaced-separated integers representing the elements of the array. The final line contains a single integer `target`.
statement.outputFormat: A boolean value indicating whether the target exists in the array.
statement.notes: The array is sorted in ascending order.

examples.explanation: The target 3 exists in the array

hints.text: Use binary search to find the target in the array

editorial.title: Editorial for Binary Search: Incorrect Early Return
editorial.intuition: The binary search algorithm works by repeatedly dividing the search interval in half. If the value of the search key is less than the item in the middle of the interval, the next interval will be the lower half. Otherwise, the next interval will be the upper half.
editorial.bruteForce: A brute force solution would involve searching the entire array, which is inefficient.
editorial.betterSolution: The binary search algorithm has a time complexity of O(log n), making it much more efficient than a brute force solution.
editorial.optimalSolution: The optimal solution uses binary search to find the target in the array.
editorial.proofOfCorrectness: The binary search algorithm works because it repeatedly divides the search interval in half until the target is found or the interval is empty.
editorial.dryRun: Step-by-step example run of the optimal solution
commonMistakes: Early return without checking the entire array
